A worked transitiontrees example: AI-prompting move sequences

This is a short, end-to-end analysis of one real dataset — not a tour of every function. The data are sequences of rhetorical moves students make while prompting an LLM (Order, Specify, Context, Reject, Request, Approve); one prompt is one sequence. The question throughout: after a given short history of moves, what does a student do next, and which contexts overturn the corpus-wide default?

1. Load and fit

context_tree() reads the long event log directly — give it the sequence id (actor), the event column (action), and the within-sequence order (order); it reshapes internally (no Nestimate/prepare_input() step needed). Prompts are short, so the deepest context worth asking for is 3 moves; with only a few hundred prompts we keep min_count low.

raw <- read.csv(
  "/Users/mohammedsaqr/Documents/Documents X/DocumentsX/TNA Work/TNA networks teem data/Seqdatalonged.csv",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

tree <- context_tree(
  raw,
  actor     = "prompt_id",   # one prompt = one sequence
  action    = "name",        # the move
  order     = "value",       # move order within a prompt
  max_depth = 3L,            # longest context the tree may hold
  min_count = 3L             # a context needs >= 3 occurrences to get a node
)
tree
## <transitiontrees>  24 nodes, depth <= 3, 6 states  [unpruned]
##   alphabet : Approve, Context, Order, Reject, Request, Specify
##   fit on   : 281 sequences, 726 observations
##   smoothing: floor(ymin=0.001, rule=interpolate)   min_count = 3
## (start)   n=726    -> Order (0.34)
## |-- Approve   n=13     -> Order (0.77)
## |-- Context   n=87     -> Specify (0.89)
## |   |-- Order     n=74     -> Specify (0.98)
## |   |   `-- Request   n=4      -> Specify (0.99)
## |   `-- Specify   n=3      -> Order (0.66)
## |-- Order     n=194    -> Specify (0.55)
## |   |-- Approve   n=10     -> Specify (0.80)
## |   |-- Context   n=3      -> Request (0.66)
## |   |-- Reject    n=14     -> Specify (0.92)
## |   `-- Request   n=17     -> Specify (0.76)
## |       `-- Reject    n=4      -> Specify (0.99)
## |-- Reject    n=56     -> Specify (0.55)
## |   `-- Request   n=3      -> Order (0.99)
## |-- Request   n=32     -> Order (0.75)
## |   |-- Context   n=3      -> Order (0.66)
## |   |-- Order     n=3      -> Specify (0.99)
## |   `-- Reject    n=5      -> Order (0.99)
## `-- Specify   n=63     -> Request (0.38)
##     |-- Context   n=18     -> Request (0.77)
##     |   `-- Order     n=14     -> Request (0.99)
##     |-- Order     n=24     -> Context (0.71)
##     |   `-- Request   n=4      -> Context (0.99)
##     `-- Reject    n=9      -> Order (0.77)

2. Inspect the model

The print banner gives depth, node count, and the corpus size. summary() adds the per-depth breakdown, and model_fit() bundles the goodness-of-fit scalars (logLik, df, nobs, AIC, BIC, perplexity) into one row.

summary(tree)
## <transitiontrees summary>  24 nodes, depth <= 3, 6 states  [unpruned]
## 
##             pathway depth count likely_next next_probability divergence
##             (start)     0   726       Order        0.3429752         NA
##               Order     1   194     Specify        0.5492371  0.8816161
##             Context     1    87     Specify        0.8921724  1.0746190
##             Specify     1    63     Request        0.3796667  1.1101873
##              Reject     1    56     Specify        0.5512500  0.4331304
##             Request     1    32       Order        0.7465000  0.6738563
##             Approve     1    13       Order        0.7656154  0.6988270
##    Order -> Context     2    74     Specify        0.9815676  0.1104970
##    Order -> Specify     2    24     Context        0.7050833  0.5941452
##  Context -> Specify     2    18     Request        0.7741111  0.7405598
##  changes_prediction
##                  NA
##                TRUE
##                TRUE
##                TRUE
##                TRUE
##               FALSE
##               FALSE
##               FALSE
##                TRUE
##               FALSE
## # ... 14 more rows (use as.data.frame(tree) for the full table)
model_fit(tree)

The in-sample perplexity of 2.76 says the fitted tree is, on average, as uncertain as a fair choice among that many moves — well below the 6-move uniform baseline, so history is genuinely informative.

3. Pathway tables

The pathway-centric API returns plain data.frames in one canonical schema (pathway | depth | count | likely_next | next_probability | divergence | changes_prediction), so every view joins cleanly. Three named verbs each fix a useful sort:

common_pathways(tree, top = 8)      # by frequency: the backbone
divergent_pathways(tree, top = 8)   # by KL from the shorter history
sharp_pathways(tree, top = 8)       # by how peaked the next-move prediction is

common_pathways() is the backbone (what students mostly do). divergent_pathways() surfaces the contexts where a longer history changes the prediction — changes_prediction = TRUE flags a modal-move flip. Those are the interesting rows: places where knowing the previous move(s) overturns the corpus default.

4. Per-context diagnostics

tree_dependence() quantifies, per context, how much the history sharpens the next-move distribution — entropy_drop is the reduction in predictive entropy (bits) the context buys over its shorter suffix.

dep <- tree_dependence(tree)
head(dep[order(-dep$entropy_drop),
         c("pathway", "depth", "count", "entropy_drop",
           "likely_before", "likely_next", "changes_prediction")], 8)

5. Prune to the reliable tree

A context that survives fitting can still be over-fit. prune_tree() collapses contexts whose extra depth does not buy a statistically significant gain over their parent (default: a likelihood-ratio test at alpha = 0.05).

pruned <- prune_tree(tree, criterion = "G2", alpha = 0.05)
pruned
## <transitiontrees>  15 nodes, depth <= 3, 6 states  [pruned]
##   alphabet : Approve, Context, Order, Reject, Request, Specify
##   fit on   : 281 sequences, 726 observations
##   smoothing: floor(ymin=0.001, rule=interpolate)   min_count = 3
##   pruned by: G2   alpha = 0.05
## (start)   n=726    -> Order (0.34)
## |-- Approve   n=13     -> Order (0.77)
## |-- Context   n=87     -> Specify (0.89)
## |   |-- Order     n=74     -> Specify (0.98)
## |   `-- Specify   n=3      -> Order (0.66)
## |-- Order     n=194    -> Specify (0.55)
## |   |-- Context   n=3      -> Request (0.66)
## |   `-- Reject    n=14     -> Specify (0.92)
## |-- Reject    n=56     -> Specify (0.55)
## |-- Request   n=32     -> Order (0.75)
## |   `-- Order     n=3      -> Specify (0.99)
## `-- Specify   n=63     -> Request (0.38)
##     |-- Context   n=18     -> Request (0.77)
##     |-- Order     n=24     -> Context (0.71)
##     `-- Reject    n=9      -> Order (0.77)

Pruning takes the model from 24 contexts down to 15 — the deepest, thinnest contexts that could not justify their own parameters are folded back into their parents.

6. Bootstrap reliability

A pathway can look informative in one dataset by chance. bootstrap_pathways() resamples whole prompts and asks which pathways are reproducibly stable (count holds within a tolerance band) and informative ( clears the bar in most resamples). A pathway worth a claim is both.

boot <- bootstrap_pathways(pruned, iter = 200L, stat = "count", seed = 1L)
boot
## <transitiontrees_bootstrap>  200 resamples
##   stability  : count in [0.50, 1.50] x observed, p < 0.05
##   informative: G^2 > qchisq(0.95, df=k-1) = 11.07, threshold 0.80
##   pathways   : 15 total, 10 stable, 8 informative, 8 both
## 
## top pathways (stable + informative first):
##             pathway depth count p_stability stability_rate stable
##               Order     1   194       0.005          1.000   TRUE
##             Context     1    87       0.005          1.000   TRUE
##             Specify     1    63       0.005          1.000   TRUE
##              Reject     1    56       0.005          1.000   TRUE
##             Request     1    32       0.005          1.000   TRUE
##    Order -> Specify     2    24       0.010          0.995   TRUE
##  Context -> Specify     2    18       0.035          0.970   TRUE
##     Reject -> Order     2    14       0.035          0.970   TRUE
##             (start)     0   726       0.005          1.000   TRUE
##    Order -> Context     2    74       0.005          1.000   TRUE
##  informative_rate informative mean_G2 ci_G2_lo ci_G2_hi
##             1.000        TRUE 244.675  220.616  271.873
##             1.000        TRUE 134.694  104.056  170.600
##             1.000        TRUE 100.891   74.014  127.689
##             1.000        TRUE  37.008   26.155   48.513
##             1.000        TRUE  32.695   19.352   50.240
##             0.985        TRUE  20.232   11.383   30.020
##             0.955        TRUE  18.618    9.901   26.992
##             0.905        TRUE  16.509    9.240   24.168
##                NA       FALSE     NaN       NA       NA
##             0.645       FALSE  12.898    4.558   21.689
## # ... 5 more pathways (use summary(x) for full table)
bs <- summary(boot)
bs[bs$stable & bs$informative,
   c("pathway", "depth", "count", "likely_next", "next_probability",
     "stability_rate", "informative_rate")]

These — the single-move backbone plus the handful of depth-2 rules that clear the reproducibility bar — are the pathways you can state as findings.

7. Plots

A few of the package’s visual outputs on this tree.

The tree. Node size scales with count, edge width with transition flow; internal-node labels sit below their circles.

plot(pruned, style = "horizontal")

Pathway heatmap. Each row is a context, each column a next move; the cell is the predicted probability, the modal cell highlighted.

plot_pathways(tree, top = 14)

Divergence lollipop. Per-context KL from the shorter history; orange marks contexts whose modal next move flips.

plot_divergence(tree, top = 12)

Bootstrap forest. Per-pathway with bootstrap CIs; the dashed line is the critical value — pathways whose interval sits entirely to its right are reproducibly informative.

plot(boot)

8. Predicting the next move

Finally, the point of the model: given a history, predict the next move and its probability.

predict(tree, c("Order", "Context"), type = "class")       # most likely next
## [1] "Specify"
predict(tree, c("Order", "Context"), type = "prob")        # full distribution
##    Approve    Context      Order     Reject    Request    Specify 
## 0.00100000 0.00100000 0.00100000 0.00100000 0.01443243 0.98156757
